editor_settings.title: Canon G7X Review: Best Photos for IG, But Is It Worth $700?
editor_settings.content:
Canon G7X Review: Best Photos for IG, But Is It Worth $700?

Yes, the Canon G7X can take the kind of photos people love on Instagram.

No, that does not automatically make it worth $700.

That is the real answer. The camera is genuinely good. The hype price is the part that gets goofy.

Quick Verdict

If you take photos constantly, care about flash-heavy nightlife shots, and want a compact camera that gives your feed more character than a phone, the G7X still makes sense.

If you just want the TikTok look and do not shoot that often, $700 is too much. At that price, you are paying for trend tax almost as much as camera quality.

Why People Still Love the G7X for Instagram

This part is not fake. The appeal is real.

The G7X line gives you a 20.1MP 1-inch sensor, a bright 24-100mm equivalent f/1.8-2.8 lens, and a built-in flash in a body that still fits in a normal bag. That combination is why the photos hit differently.

It is especially strong for:

  • night-out photos
  • dinner shots
  • sunset portraits
  • travel photos
  • fit pics with a little background still visible

The look people chase is not just sharpness. It is the mix of compact-camera flash, better low-light control than a cheap digicam, and a softer, more nostalgic feel than most phone photos.

Why the Photos Feel Better Than iPhone Shots

The G7X does not win because it is magic. It wins because it feels less sterile.

iPhone photos are fast, clean, and convenient. But they also look like iPhone photos. The G7X has more personality, especially when flash enters the picture.

Recent Reddit users summed this up pretty well. Some said the camera is overpriced right now, but others pointed out that the built-in flash is exactly why it stays popular for portraits, fit pics, beach photos, and background-heavy lifestyle shots. That part matches what people are actually posting.

If your whole goal is a more polished but still fun Instagram look without dragging around a bigger camera, the G7X absolutely delivers.

Where the G7X Gets Annoying Fast

This is where the internet hype usually leaves out the boring truth.

The G7X is not as fast or frictionless as your phone.

  • iPhone is quicker when the moment happens fast
  • you still deal with SD cards and transfers
  • autofocus can get less confident in awkward light, especially on video
  • repeat flash shooting can feel laggy

That last part matters more than people admit. If you are trying to rapid-fire a bunch of flash shots at dinner or outside a club, the G7X can start feeling slower than the price suggests it should.

It is a charming camera. It is not a friction-free camera.

Is It Actually Worth $700?

For most people, no.

That is the blunt answer.

Canon's own current catalog page still shows the G7 X Mark II and Mark III out of stock, with prices surfacing around $799.99 for the Mark II and $849.99 for the Mark III as of May 3, 2026. So if you are staring at a $700 resale listing, that does not automatically mean it is a bargain. It just means this market is weird.

The camera is worth real money. It is not worth blind money.

If you are a heavy photo person, a creator, or someone whose Instagram content genuinely matters to work or brand deals, then yes, paying up can still make sense because the camera makes your workflow easier and the results are very usable.

If you mostly want a trendy compact to use twice a month, $700 is hard to defend.

The Smarter Buy for Photo-First People

If you mainly care about photos, the smartest move is usually not paying top-dollar for a Mark III.

The Mark III makes more sense when video matters too. It adds 4K, a mic input, and more creator-focused features. But if your whole reason for wanting a G7X is flash photos, travel, nightlife, and Instagram, the older Mark II still makes a lot of sense.

That is also where value comes back into the conversation.

If you want the G7X look without paying hype pricing, this kind of Mark II listing is a much saner angle:

[product-card:canon-powershot-g7-x-mark-ii-201mp-digital-camera-7x-zoom-95-new]


That is a lot easier to defend than dropping around $700 just because social media made the camera feel rare.

One Used-Buyer Warning Nobody Mentions Enough

If you are buying a used G7X Mark II for flash photos, check the flash carefully.

Canon published a product advisory on September 19, 2024 saying a very small number of G7 X Mark II units may have a built-in flash failure caused by wiring damage after excessive pop-up use. That does not mean every used unit is risky. It does mean you should stop treating random resale listings like they are all equally safe.

For a camera that is being bought mainly for flash photos, that detail matters a lot.

What About Just Using an iPhone or Cheap Digicam?

This is the part where the G7X gets less automatic.

If you want speed, convenience, and zero hassle, the iPhone still wins.

If you want the fun, messy, nostalgic vibe and you are okay with more trial and error, an older thrifted digicam can be a much cheaper way in.

The G7X sits in the middle. Better files, better lens, better consistency, better low-light results than a random cheap digicam. More character than an iPhone. More hassle than both in different ways.

That middle ground is exactly why people love it. It is also why some people overpay for it.

Final Verdict

The Canon G7X really can give you some of the best Instagram-friendly compact-camera photos around. The built-in flash, bright lens, and 1-inch sensor still make it a very appealing little camera.

But the camera being good does not mean every price is smart.

If you shoot photos all the time and care deeply about that look, it can still be worth it. If you are asking whether it is worth $700 for the average buyer, my answer is no. At that point, you should either buy smarter, buy lower, or admit you are paying for hype as much as hardware.
